We're hiring.

We won the KR-7 humanoid platform contract. The elbow actuator does not exist yet. The demo is in six weeks.

Our robots assist in warehouse logistics, elder care, and disaster response. The joints have to work, people are depending on them.

Requirements: compact, precise, reliable. Not one of those things is optional.

What you'll do: Select a drive motor, design a gear train, tune a control loop, deliver a design spec.

Stage 0

Design Brief

Objective: Scan the hard constraints, then start the job.

Why this matters

Robots will shape the next few decades, from eldercare to prosthetics to work in places too dangerous for humans. Software is the hardest open problem, but every robot still runs on hardware. The actuator at each joint, motor and gears together, is one of the most important pieces. Torque, speed, mass, and controllability at the joint set the ceiling on what the whole machine can do.

Your mission

Design a compact elbow actuator for the KR-7. Find the torque, pick a motor, build the gears, prove the motion. The decisions here are the same ones a real Actuator Design Engineer makes.

Stakes: We can't ship a joint that needs a pit crew.

Compute in Stage 1
Torque target
60 deg/s
Minimum joint speed
60 mm
Max diameter (hard limit)
350 g
Mass budget (motor + gears)
What you’ll do, what you’ll learn  (ages 15–17, ~45 min)
You will
  • Decompose a real engineering brief into a torque target
  • Compare 5 named motors and pick one with a defended trade-off
  • Design a multi-stage gear train under 5 simultaneous constraints (torque, speed, size, mass, efficiency)
  • Tune a PID controller against a live arm simulation
  • Defend the design against a real-physics conveyor demo and a stakeholder Q&A
Skills demonstrated
  • Requirements decomposition
  • Tradeoff analysis under competing constraints
  • Parametric design with visible failure modes
  • Control fundamentals (settle time, overshoot, steady-state error)
  • Engineering communication (spec-sheet handoff)

Aligned to NGSS HS-ETS1-2/3/4 and ITEEA STEL Standards 7–8. Per-stage standards-to-evidence map in submission/standards-evidence-map.md.

Inputs for Stage 1 (payload, arm geometry)

Project: KR-7 Humanoid Robot — Upper Limb Subsystem Issued to: Junior Design Engineer (you) Priority: High — prototype deadline in 6 weeks

The joint operates as a simple revolute (hinge) joint. Forearm length: 300 mm. Forearm mass: 0.8 kg (CG at 150 mm from joint).

ParameterRequirement
Maximum payload at gripper2.0 kgThis is the number that matters
Forearm length (joint to gripper)300 mmYou'll need this for torque
Forearm mass0.8 kgDon't forget self-weight
Forearm CG distance from joint150 mm
Minimum joint speed60 deg/sFaster is better, but not at any cost
Actuator envelope (max diameter)60 mmHard limit. The arm housing is designed.
Actuator mass budget350 gIncluding gearbox
Supply voltage24 V DCOther team's problem
Operating temperature-10°C to 50°COther team's problem
Ingress protectionIP54 minimumOther team's problem

The controller and power electronics are handled by a separate team. Focus on the mechanical design — motor selection and gear train.

Stage 1

Requirements Decomposition

Engineering skill: Translating a real-world problem into a number you can design to
Dr. Chen
Find the worst-case angle. The torque number you get here decides what this robot can lift, push, or carry. Set that ceiling before picking any motor or gear.

Step 1: When is the arm working hardest?

Drag the arm and watch the torque change.

Show the math
Joint torque from a static load
τjoint(θ) = (marm · g · Lcg + mpayload · g · Larm) · cos(θ)
Sum of moments about the joint. The forearm self-weight acts at its centre of gravity (Lcg = 150 mm); the payload acts at the gripper (Larm = 300 mm). Worst case is θ = 0 (arm horizontal), where cos θ = 1. The brief uses g = 9.81 m/s².
Concrete numbers from the brief
τworst = (0.8 · 9.81 · 0.150) + (2.0 · 9.81 · 0.300) ≈ 7.06 N·m
This is the number that drives every downstream design choice.
Source: Hibbeler, Engineering Mechanics: Statics — sum of moments / free-body diagram. Standard high-school / first-year-engineering treatment.
Joint twist needed at this angle (torque) 0.00 Nm
Drag the arm to explore...
Load Sweep
Three quick positions tell you what the actuator is really fighting.
Tap a reference pose or keep dragging. The comparison board makes the worst-case load visible instead of hiding it inside a worksheet.
Current angle
Dr. Chen

Step 2: Turn the Load Into Joint Twist (Torque)

T = F₁ × d₁ + F₂ × d₂

First convert weight into push force. Then multiply by distance from the joint to get twist. That final torque number becomes the target for everything downstream.

Push From Weight (force, F = m × g)
2.0 kg × 9.81 = N
0.8 kg × 9.81 = N
Twist At The Joint (torque, T = F × d)
F₁ × 0.300 m = Nm
F₂ × 0.150 m = Nm
T₁ + T₂ = Nm

Your Engineering Requirements

RequirementValueSource
Joint twist needed (minimum torque) — Calculated from payload + forearm loads
Output speed (minimum)60 deg/sFrom design brief
Max envelope diameter60 mmFrom design brief
Max actuator mass350 gFrom design brief
Minimum efficiency85%Engineering standard (energy/heat constraint)
Downstream Pressure
That one torque number just shaped the rest of the project.
That torque number sets the motor shortlist and the gearbox load. Everything after this has to answer it.

Voltage
24V DC
Torque-Speed Curves ▼
About these motor specs

What's modeled: Each motor's torque-speed relationship is drawn as a straight line between stall torque and no-load speed at nominal voltage — the standard textbook DC-motor approximation. Continuous and peak torque values come from manufacturer datasheets.

What's simplified: Real torque-speed curves bend slightly as brush friction, temperature, and back-EMF interact. Catalog specs are at 25 °C and nominal voltage — expect to derate ~10–20% in hot environments or with battery sag. Electrical dynamics (inductance, current rise time) are ignored; the motor is treated as an ideal torque source for control purposes.

The catalog lets you compare motors on the dimensions that drive selection — torque-speed envelope, mass, efficiency. A real motor selection adds thermal margin, duty-cycle analysis, and bench testing under load.

Sources: Each motor's specs are cross-checked against the manufacturer's published datasheet (Maxon, Faulhaber, CubeMars, iPower). Verification log in docs/motor-data-verification-findings.md.

Showing 3 motors for KR-7 Elbow Joint
HINT
≥ 7.1 Nm target torque
≥ 60 deg/s joint speed
≤ 60 mm max diameter
≤ 350 g mass budget
COMPARE TRAY (pin up to two motors)
0 / 2 pinned
Pin two motors, then compare which one leaves Stage 3 with the cleaner job.

Station 01

Step 1: Choose Gearbox Type

Pick the gearbox family first.

Simple external gear mesh — one pinion drives one larger gear per stage.
Builder Mode
Spur Stack
Spur is the readable baseline: simple to reason about, but quick to get bulky when you chase ratio the lazy way.
Best At
Simple, visible ratio building and low conceptual overhead.
Watch Out For
Diameter exploding when one stage tries to do all the work.
How To Search
Find the lowest believable ratio band first. Then split the load across stages only if packaging forces you to.
Current family move: Keep the ratio honest before you add stages or tooth size.
Station 02

Step 2: Set Your Gear Reduction

Get torque and speed into range.

Choose a gear type first
10:1
2:130:160:190:1120:1

More reduction gives more torque and less speed.

Ratio trade: more torque, less speed.
Station 03

Step 3: Tune the Geometry

Now make it fit.

1
123
Stage guidance will appear here once the live geometry readout updates.
1.0 mm
0.31.01.52.02.5
Module guidance will appear here once the live geometry readout updates.
← Smaller: tighter package. Each tooth carries less load — risk of breakage at high torque. · Bigger: stronger teeth. Gears grow and crowd the envelope. →

Stages shrink diameter but add losses. Bigger teeth are stronger but larger.

Knob map — what each slider trades
RatioStep 2
↑ More joint twist · output gets slower · envelope grows · heavier · a touch less efficient
↓ Faster output · smaller package · lighter · may miss the torque target
StagesStep 3
↑ Load splits across stages · per-stage gears get smaller · slimmer envelope · more parts & mass · more meshes lose more efficiency
↓ Fewer parts · simpler build · single big gear · envelope can blow up at high ratios
ModuleStep 3
↑ Stronger teeth · headroom under load · bigger gears · heavier
↓ Smaller, lighter package · teeth may strip under high torque · tooth-stress warning may trigger

Use it when stuck: fix one red spec without breaking another.

Search Radar
Use the rails to find the closest clean geometry.
Diameter
Watching envelope
Smaller teeth and ratio splitting usually shrink the housing fastest.
Mass
Watching mass
Extra stages and bigger teeth cost material quickly.
Efficiency
Watching losses
Every extra mesh burns a little motion into heat.
Geometry trade: stages, tooth size, mass, and losses move together.
Station 04

Step 4: Review & Commit

Review the tradeoff before testing.

SC
Dr. Chen
Waiting for your first choice...
Input (motor) Output Intermediate

Output Performance

Ratio station: tune torque and speed first. Packaging checks unlock after the ratio is locked.
Show the math
Total gear ratio (multi-stage)
rtotal = r1 × r2 × … × rn
Each stage's individual ratio multiplies. Two 5:1 stages give 25:1; three 3:1 stages give 27:1.
Output torque and speed
τout = τmotor · rtotal · ηtotal   |   ωout = ωmotor / rtotal
Reduction trades speed for torque, less the per-stage efficiency η (~95% steel spur, ~96.5% planetary). For n stages, ηtotal = ηn, so a 3-stage train can lose ~14% even with good gears.
Reflected inertia at the motor
Jreflected = Jload / rtotal2
Why high-ratio actuators feel "snappier": load inertia drops with the square of the ratio. A 100:1 gearbox makes a heavy arm feel 10,000× lighter to the motor — this term is what Stage 4 PID is fighting against.
Sources: Shigley, Mechanical Engineering Design Ch. 13 (gear geometry, efficiency); Norton, Machine Design Ch. 12; standard reflected-inertia identity for rotating systems.
Joint twist out—
Target: ≥ 7.1 Nm
Joint speed out—
Target: ≥ 60 deg/s
Outer diameter—
Target: ≤ 60 mm
Total Mass—
Target: ≤ 350 g
Efficiency—
Target: ≥ 85%
About these calculations

Efficiency model: Uses published per-stage efficiency values for steel spur gears (~95%) and planetary gears (~96.5%). Real efficiency varies with load, temperature, lubrication, and surface finish. These are textbook guidelines, not measured values for a specific gearbox.

Weight model: Uses 60% fill factor for webbed spur gears and includes housing, carrier plates, and bearings for planetary sets. Real weight depends on material choice, manufacturing method, and fastener details.

Size model: Envelope diameter from pitch circles plus housing wall. Real packaging includes bearing supports, seals, and mounting features.

These simplifications let you explore the design space without getting lost in details that require FEA software and manufacturing specs. The tradeoff directions are real — the exact numbers are educational approximations.

Sources: Per-stage gear efficiency values are textbook guidelines (steel spur ~95%, planetary ~96.5%), consistent with Shigley's Mechanical Engineering Design Ch. 13 and Norton's Machine Design Ch. 12. Tooth-stress flag uses an AGMA-style bending-stress approximation. Real values require AGMA / ISO 1328 quality grading and FEA.

Tune the Controller

Three knobs control how the arm moves. Adjust each one and watch what happens.

5.0
The gas pedal — pushes harder when far from target
Run with Kp first
1.0
The brakes — resists overshoot
Unlock Damping first
0.5
The memory. Closes the "almost there" gap.

What The Motion Says

Stop time (settle time)
—
Swing past target (overshoot)
—
Final miss (steady error)
—
SC
Dr. Chen
Show the math
PID control law
u(t) = Kp · e(t) + Ki · ∫e(t) dt + Kd · de(t)/dt
where e(t) is the error (target angle − current angle) and u(t) is the commanded motor torque. Each term has a job: Kp drives toward the target, Kd damps overshoot, Ki erases steady-state error.
Second-order arm dynamics
J · θ̈ + b · θ̇ = u(t) − τload(θ)
J is the rotational inertia (your reflected inertia from Stage 3), b is viscous friction, θ̈ and θ̇ are angular acceleration and velocity, τload is the gravity / payload torque from Stage 1. This is what your gains are pushing against.
What the metric values mean
overshoot = max|θ − θtarget| / θtarget  ·  settle time = first t where |θ − θtarget| < band  ·  steady error = limt→∞ e(t)
Standard control-engineering performance metrics. The settle band is ±5% of target.
Sources: Ogata, Modern Control Engineering (PID structure, second-order dynamics); Franklin et al., Feedback Control of Dynamic Systems (performance metrics, transient response).
About this simulation

What's modeled: A second-order rotational system with PID control, using reflected inertia from your actual gear train. The arm responds to your motor's torque through your gear ratio, with realistic speed and inertia scaling.

What's simplified: Real-world actuators are messier than this. Sensors have noise. Signals get delayed. Motors have current limits. Friction wears parts down. Heat changes performance. The PID controller you are tuning here is the textbook version. Real systems add anti-windup so the integral term does not run away, filters on the derivative term to handle noise, and feedforward terms to predict instead of just react. Many real robots also chain several PID loops together. All of that goes beyond what one simple PID loop can do.

This simulation teaches why tuning matters and how P, I, and D interact. The dynamics are directionally correct — a high-inertia system really does fight overshoot harder. But professional controller design requires tools like MATLAB/Simulink and real hardware-in-the-loop testing.

Sources: Second-order rotational dynamics and PID structure follow standard introductory control texts (Ogata, Modern Control Engineering; Franklin et al., Feedback Control of Dynamic Systems). Reflected inertia computation is the standard Jreflected = Jload / N² result.

Sim-to-Real preview — how AI behaves on your hardware Conceptual visualization. Click to expand.
Sim-to-Real Preview · AI on Your Hardware
In production, robotics teams train neural-network policies in simulation before deploying to real hardware. The behaviors the AI learns are constrained by the actuator it trains on. Below is a quick preview of what an RL policy would converge to if trained on your elbow design.
Pick-and-place task · episode preview
Behavior class
—
Episodes to converge
—
Learned failure mode
—
Sim-to-real gap
—
About this preview
This is a conceptual visualization, not a real reinforcement-learning rollout. Your actuator's torque headroom, settle time, overshoot, and steady-state error are mapped to a behavior category that mirrors what an RL policy would actually converge to on similar hardware. The point: hardware quality directly shapes learned behavior. Companies like Tesla, Figure, Boston Dynamics, and Covariant treat this as a first-class design concern.
Your design journey
That five-step loop is the engineering design process. The job that does it is called Actuator Design Engineer, and it exists at every company building humanoid robots, prosthetics, surgical tools, rovers, and warehouse automation.
Requirements Decomposition Tradeoff Analysis Parametric Design Control Systems Design Review
▸  See how every choice maps to a skill
Where this design lives — 4 real industries Camera gimbal, prosthetic, humanoid joint, EV drive. Click to expand.
Where This Design Lives
The joint design you just worked on lives in things teenagers already use. The motor and gearing that picks a parcel up on an Optimus arm is the same family of design that drives a Hero Arm prosthetic finger a classmate might wear to school, the hub motor on an e-bike, and the swing on a powered wheelchair. Different scales, same five trade-offs. The 6-stage workflow you ran — requirements → motor → gears → control → review — is the loop engineers run across robotics, transportation, and medical tech.
